content.text: Routing is the pattern where an incoming request is classified and

to the agent or workflow best suited to handle it. Without routing, every request goes to a single general-purpose agent. With routing, different request types go to different specialized agents, each with a tighter system prompt and tool set. The result is
— each specialist agent has less noise to ignore.
The initial user request is classified before any agent runs
The output of one agent determines which agent runs next
LLM that reads the output and calls a dispatch tool

The simplest router is a pure Python function that inspects the request text and returns a route label. Rule-based routing adds no latency and no token cost. It is the right choice when request types are distinguishable by simple patterns.

import re

ROUTES = {
    "billing":    ["invoice", "refund", "charge", "payment", "subscription"],
    "technical":  ["error", "bug", "crash", "install", "configure", "api"],
    "sales":      ["pricing", "plan", "upgrade", "discount", "trial"],
}

def rule_based_router(request: str) -> str:
    """
    Classify a support request into a route using keyword matching.
    Returns the route name, or 'general' if no route matches.
    """
    text = request.lower()
    scores = {route: 0 for route in ROUTES}
    for route, keywords in ROUTES.items():
        for kw in keywords:
            if re.search(r"\b" + re.escape(kw) + r"\b", text):
                scores[route] += 1
    best_route = max(scores, key=scores.get)
    return best_route if scores[best_route] > 0 else "general"

# Examples:
print(rule_based_router("I was charged twice on my invoice"))    # billing
print(rule_based_router("My API key keeps returning 401"))       # technical
print(rule_based_router("Can I get a discount on the annual plan?"))  # sales
print(rule_based_router("Hello, I need some help"))              # general
The keyword router counts how many route-specific keywords appear in the request. The route with the highest count wins. If no keywords match, the route is
. This approach breaks down when requests span multiple categories (e.g., 'I have a billing error after the API returned 500') — LLM-based routing handles these better.
When requests are ambiguous, an LLM classifier produces more accurate routing decisions than keyword rules. The classifier is a
— not the same model as the downstream specialist agents. It returns only a route label, so

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SPECIALIST_AGENTS = {
    "billing": {
        "system": "You are a billing support agent. Help with invoices, refunds, and payments.",
        "tools": [],  # billing-specific tools
    },
    "technical": {
        "system": "You are a technical support agent. Help with errors, APIs, and configuration.",
        "tools": [],  # technical tools
    },
    "sales": {
        "system": "You are a sales agent. Explain pricing, plans, and upgrade options.",
        "tools": [],  # CRM tools
    },
    "general": {
        "system": "You are a general support agent. Help with any request.",
        "tools": [],
    },
}

CLASSIFIER_SYSTEM = """
Classify the user request into exactly one category.
Categories: billing, technical, sales, general.
Return only the category name — nothing else.
"""

def classify_request(request: str) -> str:
    """Return a route label using a fast LLM classifier."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        system=CLASSIFIER_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
    )
    label = resp.content[0].text.strip().lower()
    return label if label in SPECIALIST_AGENTS else "general"

def dispatch(request: str) -> str:
    """Route the request and run the appropriate specialist agent."""
    route = classify_request(request)
    agent_config = SPECIALIST_AGENTS[route]

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=agent_config["system"],
        tools=agent_config["tools"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
    )
    answer = next((b.text for b in resp.content if hasattr(b, "text")), "")
    return f"[Route: {route}]\n{answer}"

# Examples:
print(dispatch("My API call returned a 429 rate limit error"))
# [Route: technical] Here's how to handle rate limit errors...

print(dispatch("I was charged for a plan I cancelled last month"))
# [Route: billing] Let me look into that charge for you...
because the output is a single word. This makes the classification step fast and cheap. The

function calls the classifier first, then routes to the specialist. The specialist gets a focused system prompt and only the tools relevant to its domain. A billing agent that cannot see technical tools cannot accidentally attempt a code fix, and a technical agent that cannot see CRM tools cannot accidentally read billing data.
A router must handle two edge cases: requests that do not fit any known route, and requests that the dispatched agent fails to resolve. Both require

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class RoutingDispatcher:
    """
    Router with fallback to general agent and human escalation.
    """
    def __init__(self, agent_configs: dict):
        self.configs = agent_configs

    def classify(self, request: str) -> str:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=10,
            system=CLASSIFIER_SYSTEM,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
        )
        label = resp.content[0].text.strip().lower()
        return label if label in self.configs else "general"

    def run_specialist(self, route: str, request: str) -> dict:
        config = self.configs[route]
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=config["system"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
        )
        answer = next((b.text for b in resp.content if hasattr(b, "text")), "")
        resolved = "I don't know" not in answer and "I cannot" not in answer
        return {"route": route, "answer": answer, "resolved": resolved}

    def dispatch_with_fallback(self, request: str) -> dict:
        """
        Try the classified route first.
        If unresolved, try general.
        If still unresolved, escalate to human.
        """
        route = self.classify(request)
        result = self.run_specialist(route, request)

        if result["resolved"]:
            return result

        # First fallback: general agent
        if route != "general":
            result = self.run_specialist("general", request)
            if result["resolved"]:
                return result

        # Escalate to human
        return {
            "route": "human_escalation",
            "answer": None,
            "resolved": False,
            "escalation_reason": f"Specialist '{route}' and general agent could not resolve request",
            "original_request": request,
        }

dispatcher = RoutingDispatcher(SPECIALIST_AGENTS)
result = dispatcher.dispatch_with_fallback("What is the meaning of life?")
print(result["route"])  # human_escalation (no specialist covers philosophy)
The fallback chain is: specialist -> general -> human escalation. The
heuristic (checking for 'I don't know' or 'I cannot' in the answer) is a simple proxy for agent confidence. In production, replace this with a structured

field in the agent's output — a structured check is more reliable than string matching. Human escalation returns a structured dict with the failure reason, enabling the host application to open a human support ticket.
Some requests belong to more than one category. A message like 'I was charged for a plan I cancelled after the API returned an error' is both a billing and a technical issue.
classifies the request into all applicable categories and dispatches to multiple specialist agents in parallel. Their results are merged before returning to the caller.

import anthropic
import json
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

MULTI_LABEL_CLASSIFIER_SYSTEM = """
Classify the user request into one or more of these categories: billing, technical, sales, general.
A request may belong to multiple categories.
Return ONLY a JSON array of category names: ["billing", "technical"]
Return ["general"] if no specific category applies.
"""

def classify_multi_label(request: str) -> list[str]:
    """Return a list of applicable route labels."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=30,
        system=MULTI_LABEL_CLASSIFIER_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
    )
    text = resp.content[0].text.strip()
    if text.startswith("```"):
        lines = text.split("\n")
        text = "\n".join(lines[1:-1] if lines[-1].strip() == "```" else lines[1:])
    try:
        labels = json.loads(text)
        valid = [l for l in labels if l in SPECIALIST_AGENTS]
        return valid if valid else ["general"]
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        return ["general"]

def dispatch_multi_label(request: str) -> dict:
    """
    Route to all applicable specialists in parallel.
    Merge their answers into a combined response.
    """
    labels = classify_multi_label(request)

    if len(labels) == 1:
        # Single label — direct dispatch
        route = labels[0]
        config = SPECIALIST_AGENTS[route]
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=config["system"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
        )
        return {
            "labels": labels,
            "answers": {route: next((b.text for b in resp.content if hasattr(b, "text")), "")},
        }

    # Multiple labels — dispatch in parallel
    answers = {}
    def call_specialist(label: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
        config = SPECIALIST_AGENTS[label]
        r = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=config["system"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
        )
        return label, next((b.text for b in r.content if hasattr(b, "text")), "")

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(labels)) as pool:
        futures = {pool.submit(call_specialist, label): label for label in labels}
        for future in as_completed(futures):
            label, answer = future.result()
            answers[label] = answer

    # Merge answers via a synthesis call
    merge_prompt = (
        f"A user request was handled by {len(labels)} specialist agents.\n"
        f"Request: {request}\n\n"
        + "\n\n".join(f"[{label} agent]:\n{ans}" for label, ans in answers.items())
        + "\n\nCombine these answers into a single coherent response for the user."
    )
    merge_resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": merge_prompt}],
    )
    merged = next((b.text for b in merge_resp.content if hasattr(b, "text")), "")

    return {"labels": labels, "answers": answers, "merged": merged}

# Example: billing + technical request
result = dispatch_multi_label(
    "I was charged after the API returned a 500 error during checkout"
)
print(f"Labels: {result['labels']}")
# Labels: ['billing', 'technical']

of labels rather than a single string. When more than one label is returned, all matching specialists run in parallel. Their answers are merged by a synthesis call that explicitly labels each specialist's contribution. If only one label is returned, the dispatcher skips the parallel phase and the merge step, keeping the common case fast.

Routing decisions are easy to get wrong silently. A classifier that systematically mislabels a request type will cause that request type to be handled by the wrong specialist without any visible error. Logging routing decisions makes miscategorization diagnosable.

import time
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class RoutingEvent:
    request_id: str
    request_preview: str  # first 80 chars
    labels_assigned: list[str]
    classifier_latency_ms: float
    specialist_latency_ms: dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict)
    final_status: str = "pending"  # "resolved" | "escalated" | "failed"

def dispatch_with_logging(
    request: str,
    request_id: str,
    event_log: list,
) -> dict:
    """
    Dispatch a request, logging a RoutingEvent for every decision.
    """
    # Classify
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    labels = classify_multi_label(request)
    classifier_ms = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000

    event = RoutingEvent(
        request_id=request_id,
        request_preview=request[:80],
        labels_assigned=labels,
        classifier_latency_ms=round(classifier_ms, 1),
    )

    # Dispatch specialists
    answers = {}
    for label in labels:
        config = SPECIALIST_AGENTS[label]
        t1 = time.monotonic()
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=config["system"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": request}],
        )
        specialist_ms = (time.monotonic() - t1) * 1000
        event.specialist_latency_ms[label] = round(specialist_ms, 1)
        answers[label] = next((b.text for b in resp.content if hasattr(b, "text")), "")

    event.final_status = "resolved"
    event_log.append(event)

    return {"labels": labels, "answers": answers}

# Collect routing events for analysis:
log = []
dispatch_with_logging("My billing invoice shows a wrong amount", "req-001", log)
dispatch_with_logging("API returns 401 after I rotate keys", "req-002", log)

for e in log:
    print(f"{e.request_id}: {e.labels_assigned}, "
          f"classify={e.classifier_latency_ms}ms")
# req-001: ['billing'], classify=120.4ms
# req-002: ['technical'], classify=115.2ms

dataclass captures the assigned labels, classifier latency, and per-specialist latency. Aggregating these events reveals classifier accuracy (if ground truth is known), latency distributions per route, and escalation rates per category. A billing route with a 20% escalation rate signals either that the classifier is routing non-billing requests to the billing specialist, or that the billing specialist's system prompt needs improvement.
The right routing strategy depends on how many categories exist, how cleanly they separate, and what latency budget is available:
Categories map cleanly to distinct vocabulary; fast path required
Requests often span topic boundaries; accuracy matters more than speed
1 cheap model call + N specialist calls
Requests can belong to multiple categories; merging is acceptable
Keyword rules first; LLM only for unmatched
High request volume; most requests fit known patterns

The hybrid strategy is the most common in production. Keyword rules handle the majority of high-volume, predictable requests instantly. The LLM classifier handles the tail of ambiguous requests. The breakeven point: if keyword rules match more than 70% of requests, the hybrid approach is cheaper than running the LLM on every request.
Routing dispatches each request to the specialized agent best equipped to handle it.

Keyword rules are fast and zero-cost; use them when request types map cleanly to patterns. LLM classifiers handle ambiguous requests at the cost of a small additional model call. Specialist agents get focused system prompts and only the tools their domain requires. Always define a fallback: a general agent for unclassified requests and a human escalation path for requests no agent can resolve.
